lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: I warrant it is to knight you, captain.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: God’s will and his pleasure, captain, I beseech you now, come apace

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: to the King. There is more good toward you peradventure than is

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: in your knowledge to dream of.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: Sir, know you this glove?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Know the glove! I know the glove is a glove.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: I know this; and thus I challenge it.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: ’Sblood! an arrant traitor as any is in the universal world, or

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: in France, or in England!

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: How now, sir! you villain!

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: Do you think I’ll be forsworn?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Stand away, Captain Gower; I will give treason his payment into plows, I warrant you.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: I am no traitor.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: That’s a lie in thy throat. I charge you in his majesty’s

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: name, apprehend him: he’s a friend of the Duke Alençon’s.

lines.speaker: WARWICK
lines.text: How now, how now! what’s the matter?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: My lord of Warwick, here is—praised be God for it!—a most contagious

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: treason come to light, look you, as you shall desire in a

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: summer’s day. Here is his majesty.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: How now! what’s the matter?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: My liege, here is a villain and a traitor, that, look your

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: grace, has struck the glove which your majesty is take out of

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the helmet of Alençon.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: My liege, this was my glove; here is the fellow of it;

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: and he that I gave it to in change promised to wear

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: it in his cap: I promised to strike him, if he did:

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: I met this man with my glove in his cap, and I

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: have been as good as my word.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Your majesty hear now, saving your majesty’s manhood, what an arrant, rascally,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: beggarly, lousy knave it is: I hope your majesty is pear me

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: testimony and witness, and will avouchment, that this is the glove of

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Alençon, that your majesty is give me; in your conscience, now.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Give me thy glove, soldier: look, here is the fellow of it.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: ’Twas I, indeed, thou promised’st to strike;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And thou hast given me most bitter terms.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: An it please your majesty, let his neck answer for it, if

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: there is any martial law in the world.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: How canst thou make me satisfaction?

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: All offences, my lord, come from the heart: never came any from

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: mine that might offend your majesty.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: It was ourself thou didst abuse.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: Your majesty came not like yourself: you appeared to me but as

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: a common man; witness the night, your garments, your lowliness; and what

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: your highness suffered under that shape, I beseech you take it for

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: your own fault and not mine: for had you been as I

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: took you for, I made no offence; therefore, I beseech your highness,

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: pardon me.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Here, uncle Exeter, fill this glove with crowns,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And give it to this fellow. Keep it, fellow;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And wear it for an honour in thy cap

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Till I do challenge it. Give him his crowns:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And, captain, you must needs be friends with him.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: By this day and this light, the fellow has mettle enough in

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: his belly. Hold, there is twelve pence for you; and I pray

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: you to serve God, and keep you out of prawls, and prabbles,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: and quarrels, and dissensions, and, I warrant you, it is the better

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: for you.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: I will none of your money.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: It is with a good will; I can tell you, it will

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: serve you to mend your shoes: come, wherefore should you be so

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: pashful? your shoes is not so good: ’tis a good silling, I

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: warrant you, or I will change it.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Now, herald, are the dead number’d?

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: Here is the number of the slaughter’d French.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What prisoners of good sort are taken, uncle?

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Charles Duke of Orleans, nephew to the king;

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: John Duke of Bourbon, and Lord Bouciqualt:

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Of other lords and barons, knights and squires,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Full fifteen hundred, besides common men.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: This note doth tell me of ten thousand French

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That in the field lie slain: of princes, in this number,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And nobles bearing banners, there lie dead

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: One hundred twenty-six: added to these,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Of knights, esquires, and gallant gentlemen,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Eight thousand and four hundred; of the which,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Five hundred were but yesterday dubb’d knights:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: So that, in these ten thousand they have lost,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: There are but sixteen hundred mercenaries;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The rest are princes, barons, lords, knights, squires,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And gentlemen of blood and quality.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The names of those their nobles that lie dead:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Charles Delabreth, high constable of France;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Jacques of Chatillon, admiral of France;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The master of the crossbows, Lord Rambures;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Great Master of France, the brave Sir Guichard Dolphin,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: John, Duke of Alençon, Anthony Duke of Brabant,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The brother to the Duke of Burgundy,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And Edward Duke of Bar: of lusty earls,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Grandpré and Roussi, Fauconberg and Foix,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Beaumont and Marle, Vaudemont and Lestrale.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Here was a royal fellowship of death!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Where is the number of our English dead? Herald shews him another paper.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Edward the Duke of York, the Earl of Suffolk,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Sir Richard Ketly, Davy Gam, esquire:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: None else of name; and of all other men

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But five and twenty. O God, thy arm was here;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And not to us, but to thy arm alone,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Ascribe we all! When, without stratagem,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But in plain shock and even play of battle,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Was ever known so great and little loss

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: On one part and on the other? Take it, God,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For it is none but thine!

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: ’Tis wonderful!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Come, go we in procession to the village:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And be it death proclaimed through our host

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To boast of this or take that praise from God

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Which is his only.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Is it not lawful, an please your majesty, to tell how many is killed?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Yes, captain; but with this acknowledgment,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That God fought for us.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Yes, my conscience, he did us great good.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Do we all holy rites;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Let there be sung “Non nobis” and “Te Deum;”

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The dead with charity enclosed in clay:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And then to Calais; and to England then;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Where ne’er from France arrived more happy men.

glosses.definition: Williams thinks Fluellen is being summoned for knighthood

glosses.phrase: Know the glove! I know the glove is a glove
glosses.definition: Fluellen's comic tautology
'by God's blood' — a strong oath
punish treason with blows — Fluellen's 'plows' = 'blows'
not to impugn your majesty's courage — Fluellen's polite hedge as he insults the 'traitor'
poverty-stricken, lice-ridden scoundrel
affirmation; certification — Fluellen's mock-legal word
Henry admits Williams spoke harshly to him in 4.1 — plays at being offended

glosses.phrase: let his neck answer for it
glosses.definition: hang him, in Fluellen's view

glosses.phrase: All offences, my lord, come from the heart
glosses.definition: Williams's moral defence — no offence was intended
you appeared as a common soldier, not a king — Williams's self-defence
your borrowed cloak and lowly bearing
a purse in the shape of a glove — Henry's way of rewarding Williams's honesty
a shilling — Fluellen's small personal gift to Williams
brawls and brabbles (squabbles), in Fluellen's accent — dialect preserved
bashful, in Fluellen's accent
shilling, in Fluellen's accent
the casualty list — Henry will read it aloud, producing the play's roll-call of dead nobles
Charles d'Orléans — captured at Agincourt, held in England for 25 years; a noted poet in captivity
John I, Duke of Bourbon — captured at Agincourt; died in English captivity 1434
Boucicaut — Marshal of France; captured at Agincourt, died in English captivity
the play's inflated casualty figure — the historical estimate is ~6,000–10,000 French dead at Agincourt
the number of French princes, dukes and banner-bearing nobles dead — precision lends the catalogue authority
500 who had been newly knighted on the eve of battle — all killed
1,600 of the dead were hired soldiers — the rest aristocrats, a deliberate inversion of normal casualty ratios
Charles d'Albret — Constable of France, killed at Agincourt (already in 3.5)
Admiral of France — killed
Guichard Dauphin — 'Great Master of France'; killed
French duke killed by Henry personally — note, Alençon as Henry's own kill

glosses.phrase: Brother to the Duke of Burgundy
glosses.definition: the Duke of Brabant — related to the Duke of Burgundy (who was not at Agincourt)
an assembly of nobility united only by dying — Henry's phrase for the catalogue
Kettle/Kethele and Dafydd Gam — actual English casualties, one Welsh; 'Davy Gam' = the historical David Gam, killed saving Henry's life (per Tudor chroniclers)
no other English nobles dead — the vanishingly small English loss
25 English dead (common soldiers) — historically an understatement, but propaganda had already inflated the French dead and deflated the English
God's arm fought for us — Henry's providentialism
without trickery; in straight combat

glosses.phrase: plain shock and even play of battle
glosses.definition: straightforward clash of arms

glosses.phrase: boast of this or take that praise from God
glosses.definition: Henry forbids boasting — all credit to God; proclamation on pain of death
Latin Psalm 115 — 'Non nobis, Domine': 'Not unto us, O Lord, but unto thy name give glory.' Traditional English battle-thanksgiving
'Te Deum laudamus' — the ancient Latin hymn of thanksgiving to God
buried charitably in the earth
the English-held Channel port — Henry's destination, already planned at the end of 3.3

annotations.title: The glove-plot pays off — Henry gives Williams crowns
annotations.body: The glove-exchange set up in 4.1 pays off here. Williams has punched Fluellen (believing the glove in Fluellen's cap belongs to the arrogant soldier who insulted the king); Henry reveals that he was that soldier. Williams's defense is disarmingly direct: 'Your majesty came not like yourself … had you been as I took you for, I made no offence.' Henry's response is generous: he fills Williams's glove with crowns. What makes the sequence work is Williams's refusal to apologize: the king in disguise invited the insult, and the responsibility is not on the soldier. Shakespeare does not flinch from having Williams win the argument. Henry rewards him with money, not with a title; Williams will go home as a commoner, which is the appropriate register. The king-in-disguise trope is a favorite of Elizabethan drama (it recurs in As You Like It and Measure for Measure); Shakespeare's version here is unusual in that the commoner gets to keep his moral advantage.
sources: Anne Barton, 'The King Disguised,' in The Triple Bond (1975)
Kenneth Muir, The Sources of Shakespeare's Plays (1977)

annotations.title: The catalogue of the dead — historical politics of a casualty report
annotations.body: Henry's long recitation of the French noble dead is a set-piece of propagandist accounting. The numbers are unreliable: 10,000 French dead, 126 princes and banner-bearing nobles, 8,400 knights and gentlemen, 500 freshly-knighted — and only 25 common English dead plus four named nobles (York, Suffolk, Kettle, Davy Gam). Historical estimates of Agincourt casualties vary considerably, but modern historians generally put French losses at roughly 6,000–10,000 (heavily weighted toward the nobility, who led the fatal cavalry charge into the mud) and English losses at probably several hundred rather than 25. Shakespeare follows his source (Holinshed) but the source itself inherits the aftermath-propaganda. What the catalogue accomplishes dramatically is different from its historical accuracy: it stages an astonishing inversion of normal war-casualties (nobles killed in massive numbers, commoners almost untouched) and it invites the audience to hear 'God fought for us' as both historically adequate (in the sense that such a disproportion is not ordinary) and theologically exorbitant (since attributing it wholly to God erases the strategic fact of the longbow). Henry's immediate decree banning boasting on pain of death — 'be it death proclaimed through our host / To boast of this or take that praise from God / Which is his only' — closes the catalogue by making providentialism state policy.
sources: Anne Curry, Agincourt: A New History (2005)
Juliet Barker, Agincourt: The King, the Campaign, the Battle (2005)
Peter Saccio, Shakespeare's English Kings (2nd ed., 2000)

options: Williams is imprisoned for striking Fluellen
Williams explains that the king's disguise invited the insult; Henry, persuaded, fills Williams's glove with crowns. Williams doesn't apologize; the king doesn't insist
Fluellen is rewarded with a dukedom
Both Williams and Fluellen are sent to Calais

trials.prompt: According to Henry's list, how many common English soldiers died?
options: Twenty-five — an astonishingly low number that has been disputed by modern historians, who estimate several hundred

trials.prompt: What does Henry do immediately after reciting the casualty catalogue?
options: Leads a prayer at the battlefield
Issues a decree — on pain of death — forbidding any soldier to boast of Agincourt or take credit from God. Providentialism becomes state policy
Orders the dead to be burned
Writes a personal letter to Katherine

trials.prompt: How should readers treat Henry's casualty numbers?
options: As propaganda inherited from 15th-century sources (via Holinshed): the disproportion (French nobles killed in huge numbers, English commoners barely scratched) is extraordinary and real, but the specific numbers are likely inflated/deflated on both sides. Modern estimates differ substantially
As literal transcripts of French rolls
As inventions of Shakespeare with no historical basis
